perturbation.original_text: (a) "Contract Period" shall mean that period of time commencing on January 1, 2000 and concluding December 31, 2003, unless terminated sooner as provided herein.
perturbation.changed_text: (a) "Contract Period" shall mean that period of time commencing on January 1, 2000 and concluding December 31, 2003, regardless of any termination clauses provided herein.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that the contract period is fixed regardless of termination clauses, it directly contradicts the 'unless terminated sooner as provided herein' clause, creating uncertainty about the actual duration of the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 1302.93 allows for contract termination based on specific terms agreed upon by both parties. By stating the contract period continues regardless of termination clauses, the modification contradicts the statutory right to enforce agreed-upon termination provisions, making the contract unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1302.93 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1302.93 | Contractual modification or limitation of remedy - UCC 2-719. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1302 Sales Effective: August 15, 1996 Latest Legislation: Senate Bill 155 - 121st General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF (A) Subject to the provisions of divisions (B) and (C) of this section and of section 1302.92 of the Revised Code on liquidation and limitation of damages, both of the following apply: (1) The agreement may provide for remedies in addition to or in substitution for those provided in sections 1302.01 to 1302.98 of the Revised Code and may limit or alter the measure of damages recoverable under those sections, as by limiting the buyer's remedies to return of the goods and repayment of the price or to repair and replacement of nonconforming goods or parts. (2) Resort to a remedy as provided is optional unless the remedy is expressly agreed to be exclusive, in which case it is the sole remedy. (B) Where circumstances cause an exclusive or limited remedy to fail of its essential purpose, remedy may be had as provided in Chapters 1301., 1302., 1303., 1304., 1305., 1307., 1308., 1309., and 1310. of the Revised Code. (C) Consequential damages may be limited or excluded unless the limitation or exclusion is unconscionable. Limitation of consequential damages for injury to the person in the case of consumer goods is prima facie unconscionable, but limitation of damages where the loss is commercial is not. Available Versions of this Section August 15, 1996 – Senate Bill 155 - 121st General Assembly [ View August 15, 1996 Version ]
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction by stating that the contract period continues regardless of termination clauses, directly opposing the original clause ('unless terminated sooner as provided herein'). This undermines the enforceability of termination provisions. Ohio Revised Code § 1302.93(A)(1), as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that 'The agreement may provide for remedies in addition to or in substitution for those provided in sections 1302.01 to 1302.98 of the Revised Code and may limit or alter the measure of damages recoverable under those sections...'. By making the contract period fixed, the changed text contradicts the ability to limit remedies or alter damage measures via termination as explicitly permitted by this section of the Ohio Revised Code.

perturbation.original_text: Past due payments hereunder shall bear interest at the rate of (a) one and one-half percent (1-1/2%) per month, or (b) the maximum interest rate permissible under law, whichever is less.
perturbation.changed_text: Past due payments hereunder shall bear interest at the rate of (a) one and one-half percent (1-1/2%) per month, or (b) a rate determined at the Company's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause limits the interest rate to the legal maximum. This change allows the Company to set the interest rate, potentially exceeding legal limits and violating usury laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 1343.01 establishes the legal interest rate limits. Allowing the Company to determine the interest rate at its discretion contradicts this law, as the rate could exceed the legal limit, making the clause unenforceable and potentially subjecting the company to penalties.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1343.01 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1343.01 | Maximum rate of interest. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1343 Interest Effective: June 29, 1988 Latest Legislation: Senate Bill 130 - 117th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF (A) The parties to a bond, bill, promissory note, or other instrument of writing for the forbearance or payment of money at any future time, may stipulate therein for the payment of interest upon the amount thereof at any rate not exceeding eight per cent per annum payable annually, except as authorized in division (B) of this section. (B) Any party may agree to pay a rate of interest in excess of the maximum rate provided in division (A) of this section when: (1) The original amount of the principal indebtedness stipulated in the bond, bill, promissory note, or other instrument of writing exceeds one hundred thousand dollars; (2) The payment is to a broker or dealer registered under the "Securities Exchange Act of 1934," 48 Stat. 881, 15 U.S.C. 78A, as amended, for carrying a debit balance in an account for a customer if such debit balance is payable on demand and secured by stocks, bonds or other securities; (3) The instrument evidences a loan secured by a mortgage or deed of trust on real estate where the loan has been approved, insured, guaranteed, purchased, or for which an offer or commitment to insure, guarantee, or purchase has been received, in whole or in part, by the federal government or any agency or instrumentality thereof, the federal national mortgage association, the federal home loan mortgage corporation, or the farmers home administration, all of which is authorized pursuant to the "National Housing Act,"
perturbation.justification: The original contract limited interest rates to the legal maximum, while the changed version allows the Company to set rates at its discretion. This contradicts Ohio Revised Code § 1343.01, which sets a maximum interest rate. Snippet 1 states, "The parties to a bond, bill, promissory note, or other instrument of writing for the forbearance or payment of money at any future time, may stipulate therein for the payment of interest upon the amount thereof at any rate not exceeding eight per cent per annum payable annually..." By allowing the company to set the interest rate without this limit, the changed text creates a direct legal contradiction with the law. The contract change removes a core legal obligation, making the contract likely to fail legal scrutiny.

perturbation.original_text: Company agrees to protect, indemnify and save harmless Licensor, Duval and their authorized agent, or any of them, from and against any and all expenses, damages, claims, suits, actions, judgments and costs whatsoever, including reasonable attorneys' fees, arising out of, or in any way connected with, actions or omissions of Company, any advertising material furnished by, or on behalf of, Company or any claim or action for personal injury, death or other cause of action involving alleged defects in Company's Products or services.
perturbation.changed_text: Company agrees to protect, indemnify and save harmless Licensor, Duval and their authorized agent, or any of them, from and against any and all expenses, damages, claims, suits, actions, judgments and costs whatsoever, arising out of, or in any way connected with, actions or omissions of Company, regardless of whether those actions or omissions constituted gross negligence or willful misconduct on the part of the Licensor, Duval, or their authorized agent.
perturbation.explanation: The original indemnification clause is standard. The modification requires the Company to indemnify the Licensor even for the Licensor's own gross negligence or willful misconduct, which is often unenforceable under law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Indemnification limitations for gross negligence/willful misconduct
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 2305.31 generally prohibits indemnity agreements that protect a party from their own gross negligence or willful misconduct. The modification requiring Mossimo to indemnify David Duval enterprises from their own harmful actions directly contradicts this limitation, rendering the indemnification clause potentially unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 2305.31 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 2305.31 | Promisee indemnified against damage liability. Ohio Revised Code / Title 23 Courts-Common Pleas / Chapter 2305 Jurisdiction; Limitation Of Actions Effective: November 19, 1975 Latest Legislation: House Bill 489 - 111th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF A covenant, promise, agreement, or understanding in, or in connection with or collateral to, a contract or agreement relative to the design, planning, construction, alteration, repair, or maintenance of a building, structure, highway, road, appurtenance, and appliance, including moving, demolition, and excavating connected therewith, pursuant to which contract or agreement the promisee, or its independent contractors, agents or employees has hired the promisor to perform work, purporting to indemnify the promisee, its independent contractors, agents, employees, or indemnitees against liability for damages arising out of bodily injury to persons or damage to property initiated or proximately caused by or resulting from the negligence of the promisee, its independent contractors, agents, employees, or indemnitees is against public policy and is void. Nothing in this section shall prohibit any person from purchasing insurance from an insurance company authorized to do business in the state of Ohio for his own protection or from purchasing a construction bond. Available Versions of this Section November 19, 1975 – House Bill 489 - 111th General Assembly [ View November 19, 1975 Version ]
perturbation.justification: The modified text requires the Company to indemnify the Licensor even for the Licensor's own gross negligence or willful misconduct. The Law Explanation states that Ohio Revised Code § 2305.31 generally prohibits indemnity agreements that protect a party from their own gross negligence or willful misconduct. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this by stating that agreements purporting to indemnify against liability for damages arising from the negligence of the promisee are against public policy and void. Therefore, the modified contract text directly contradicts Ohio law, creating a clear legal contradiction.
